title: Targetprocess hierarchy, projects & API
topic: IBM Targetprocess (formerly Apptio Targetprocess) — data hierarchy, projects, programs, teams, and API/data model. Secondary pass: VersionOne (Digital.ai Agility).
summary: Targetprocess uses a 5-level fixed work hierarchy (Portfolio Epic→Epic→Feature→Story→Task/Bug), project-scoped entities, program groupings, a flat REST API v1 with OData-style querying, and process-based configuration without true custom entity types.
content:
Contents
  1. Summary
  2. 1. Default Entity Hierarchy
  3. 2. Storage Model
  4. 3. Custom Entity Types
  5. 4. Parent-Child Mechanics
  6. 5. Rollups
  7. 6. Reparenting
  8. 7. Subtree Filtering / View System
  9. 8–10. Project, Program, and Their Relationship
  10. 11. What Lives Inside a Project?
  11. 12. Cross-Project Work
  12. 13. Team Entity
  13. 14. User Membership
  14. 15. Permissions
  15. 16–17. REST API Model
  16. 18. Webhooks / Automation Rules
  17. Comparison: Targetprocess vs. VersionOne (Digital.ai Agility)
  18. Sources
  19. Gaps and Limitations

Summary

Targetprocess (rebranded as IBM Targetprocess / Apptio Targetprocess after IBM's 2023 acquisition of Apptio) organises work in a fixed five-level hierarchy: Portfolio Epic > Epic > Feature > User Story > Task / Bug. All entities are Project-scoped. Programs are a lightweight grouping tag above Projects. The REST API v1 exposes each entity type as a distinct resource (e.g. /api/v1/Epics) with OData-style where/include/append query parameters. IBM's official documentation (ibm.com/docs/en/targetprocess) returns HTTP 403 to non-authenticated callers; all primary source content below was obtained from the public Targetprocess guide at targetprocess.com/guide and the developer hub at dev.targetprocess.com (which redirects to IBM docs but was supplemented by cached/crawlable content and GitHub examples). Sources numbered at end of report.

1. Default Entity Hierarchy

The hierarchy has five named levels plus cross-cutting planning and test entities [S1, S2, S3, S4, S5]:

LevelEntity TypeColourNotes
0 (top)Portfolio Epic–Added v3.13.0; SAFe portfolio-level initiative; cannot directly contain Stories/Bugs
1EpicCyanSolution capability; optional level; can be renamed (Initiative, Goal, etc.)
2FeatureGreenART-level item; required for medium/large projects; each Feature belongs to one Epic (optional) and one Project
3User Story–Team-level; always one Project; optionally one Feature; inherits Epic via Feature
3 (peer)BugLight redCan parent to Feature, User Story, or both; effort excluded from parent rollups
4Task–Smallest increment (<16 h); child of User Story; effort rolls up to Story

Additionally the following entity types exist outside the primary chain [S5, S6, S7]:

  • Requests — Service Desk items from external users; convertible to User Stories
  • Test Plan / Test Case / Test Plan Run — QA entities; linked to Stories/Features
  • Release / Planning Interval — Cross-project time-boxing (8–12 weeks)
  • Iteration / Sprint — Project-level sprint container
  • Team Iteration — Team-scoped sprint; separate from Project Iteration
  • Milestone — Visual timeline flag; not a work container
  • Build — Software delivery artifact
  • Impediment / Blocker — Risk / dependency tracking
  • Relations — Generic dependency links between any two entities
  • OKR entities — Objectives (Ultimate/Strategic/Tactical), Key Results, Indicators
  • Retrospective entities — Feedbacks, Action Items, Voting
  • Risk entities — Risks, Risk Actions
  • Financial entities — Budgets, Rates, Costs, Expenses (SAFe budgeting)
  • Capacity entities — Headcount, Work Allocations, Demand, Availability

2. Storage Model

Targetprocess does not publish its internal database schema publicly. Based on API evidence, each entity type is exposed as a separate named resource (/api/v1/UserStories, /api/v1/Features, /api/v1/Epics, /api/v1/PortfolioEpics, etc.) rather than a single polymorphic entity table [S8, S9]. This strongly implies separate tables (or at minimum separate typed views) per entity type. The API exposes a common set of scalar fields (Id, Name, Description, Effort, CreateDate, ModifyDate, EntityState) plus type-specific relational fields. There is no documented generic "entity" endpoint — every type is addressed by its own collection name. The OData-style query language is applied per-resource.

3. Custom Entity Types

Targetprocess does not support creating fully new custom entity types that can be slotted arbitrarily into the hierarchy [S10, S11]. Customisation is limited to:

  • Rename terms (per-Process): any built-in entity type can be given a custom singular/plural name and icon (e.g. "Epic" → "Initiative"). Restricted terms like "Steps" are reserved. Renamed terms appear throughout the UI but internal API names remain unchanged [S10].
  • Custom fields: global or per-Process fields added to existing entity types (text, dropdown, number, date, etc.) [S11].
  • Process configuration: enable/disable practices per project (Bug Tracking, Epics/Features, Test Cases, Time Tracking, Help Desk, Source Control) — this controls which entity types are active in that Project's process [S12].
  • Workflows: per-entity, per-process state definitions and transition rules.

A public service-desk request (ID #145126) for "create custom entities, custom hierarchy" was filed by users — the request page was unreachable (ECONNREFUSED) at crawl time, but its existence confirms this is a requested but not yet delivered capability [S13].

4. Parent-Child Mechanics

Each entity holds a direct FK-style single-value reference to its parent entity of the next type up. The API exposes these as first-class fields on the entity object [S8, S9]:

  • User Story → Feature (optional single-value FK: Feature.Id)
  • Feature → Epic (optional single-value FK: Epic.Id)
  • Epic → Portfolio Epic (optional single-value FK: PortfolioEpic.Id)
  • Bug → Feature (optional) AND/OR Bug → User Story (optional); the dual-parent is explicitly supported. A Bug's parent Feature and its parent Story's parent Feature may be two different Features [S4].
  • Task → User Story (required single-value FK)

Every entity also carries a required Project.Id FK. Epic inheritance is not stored redundantly: a User Story does not hold an Epic.Id field directly — it inherits Epic via its Feature. API queries must traverse: ?include=[Feature[Epic]] to surface it [S9].

Sub-Epics (Epics inside Epics) are not supported. The only way to get a two-level Epic structure is to use Portfolio Epics (level 0) and Epics (level 1) — there is no arbitrary recursive self-referencing. Portfolio Epics are a distinct type, not a polymorphic parent pointer [S3].

5. Rollups

Effort and progress aggregations are computed dynamically on read rather than stored denormalised values [S14, S15]. The formula is consistent at every level:

Progress = SUM(EffortCompleted) / SUM(Effort)

Rollup chain:

ParentRolls up fromBug effort included?
User StoryTasksNo — Bug effort excluded from Story totals
FeatureUser Stories (and their Tasks)No — Bug effort excluded from Feature totals
EpicFeaturesInherited exclusion
Portfolio EpicEpics + Features directly under itInherited exclusion
Iteration / SprintUser Stories + Bugs (excluding standalone Tasks)Yes at this level

Direct time/effort entry at Feature, Epic, or Portfolio Epic level is not permitted; all effort must be entered at User Story or Task level and aggregated upward [S2, S3, S14].

6. Reparenting

Moving a User Story between Features is supported by editing the Feature field on the Story. The parent Feature may reside in a different Project than the Story — cross-project parent links are explicitly permitted [S6].

Moving a Feature to a different Project: since v3.11.3, child User Stories automatically follow the Feature to the new Project, provided the Stories were originally in the same Project as the Feature. Stories already in a different Project do not move [S16].

Moving an Epic to a different Project: child Features, User Stories, Bugs, and Tasks do not automatically follow. This creates "orphaned" cross-project hierarchical links. Targetprocess documents this limitation and recommends custom filter views to detect orphans plus Custom Rules to prevent future occurrences; existing orphans must be corrected manually [S16].

Entities can also be converted between types (e.g. Bug → User Story, User Story → Feature), which effectively reparents them in the hierarchy [S17].

7. Subtree Filtering / View System

Targetprocess provides three view modes: Board (kanban-style with swimlanes), List (hierarchical drill-down), and Timeline/Roadmap [S18, S19].

Filtering uses two modes:

  • Basic mode: point-and-click field selectors.
  • Advanced DSL mode: invoked with ? prefix; supports dot-notation hierarchy traversal:
?Feature.Epic is 'Epic 1'
?Feature.Epic.Name is 'Epic 1'
?Bugs.Where("zoom" in Name)

Operators: is, is not, ==, <, >, >=, <=, in, not in, contains(), Where, Count [S20].

Visual Encoding uses the same DSL syntax as filters to apply colour rules to cards in views — colour-coding entities based on hierarchy conditions (e.g. all Stories in a specific Epic shown in red) [S21].

The Advanced DSL does not work in REST API queries or Search — it is view/report scoped only [S20].

8–10. Project, Program, and Their Relationship

In current Targetprocess terminology the word "Portfolio" is often used interchangeably with "Project" in UI labels (the entity list shows "Portfolios (renamed Project)"), but the underlying entity type is Project [S1, S5].

ConceptInternal nameRole
Portfolio / ProjectProjectPrimary work container; all work items require a Project FK; permission boundary
ProgramProgramGrouping tag above Projects; "a custom way to group projects, working as some sort of tag"; one Project → exactly one Program (1:1 upward)

Program ↔ Project relationship: Many Projects to one Program (a Program can contain many Projects; each Project belongs to at most one Program). This is a strict single-parent rule — a Project cannot span Programs [S5, S22].

Programs are the highest organisational level. They are used for board/list/timeline views across all their member Projects. Work items cannot be directly assigned to a Program; Programs surface aggregated data from their Projects.

11. What Lives Inside a Project?

Project-scoped entities (require a Project FK) [S1, S5, S6]:

  • User Stories, Tasks, Bugs, Features, Epics, Portfolio Epics
  • Iterations / Sprints, Releases / Planning Intervals
  • Requests (if Help Desk practice enabled)
  • Test Plans, Test Cases (if Testing practice enabled)
  • Builds, Impediments, Milestones

Global / non-Project-scoped entities:

  • Programs, Teams, Users, Requesters
  • OKR Objectives / Key Results (span Programs)
  • Agile Release Trains, Solution Trains, Value Streams
  • Financial year/quarter structures (Budgets, Rates)

12. Cross-Project Work

Hierarchy can intentionally span project boundaries [S6, S16]:

  • A Feature in Project A may have a parent Epic in Project B (and vice versa).
  • A User Story in Project A may have a parent Feature in Project B.
  • These cross-project links are permitted but can create orphaned chains if a parent is moved.
  • Cross-project Releases allow Features, Stories, and Bugs from multiple projects to be assigned to a single Release for portfolio-level tracking [S23].

13. Team Entity

Team is a first-class global entity (not Project-scoped) with its own membership list separate from Project membership [S5, S24].

Iteration vs. Team Iteration:

TypeScopePurpose
Iteration (Sprint)Project-levelPlanning and tracking for a project; shared across all teams in that project
Team Iteration (Team Sprint)Team-levelTracks entire team progress; can span multiple Projects; batch-assign work items to team

A Team may be assigned to multiple Projects. A Project may have multiple Teams. The model is many-to-many at the Team ↔ Project assignment level [S5, S24].

14. User Membership

A user can be a member of multiple Projects and multiple Teams simultaneously [S5]. Access is governed by the intersection of project membership and team membership:

Project member?Team member?Visibility
YesNoAll work items in that project
NoYesOnly work assigned to their team within accessible projects
YesYesFull access to all project work
NoNoNo access

15. Permissions

Four user account types exist, each with a different permission ceiling [S25]:

  • Administrator: full access to all active public projects; manages process settings, terms, workflows, users.
  • User: standard team member; access via project/team membership; role within membership controls add/edit/delete.
  • Observer: read-access to all public projects; limited create/edit/delete.
  • Contributor: accesses only projects/teams they join; permissions per assigned role.

Within User/Observer/Contributor accounts, each membership record carries a Role that controls which entity types they can view/add/edit/delete in that specific Project or Team. Roles are configured per-Process. There is no row-level security beyond the membership + role model, except that creators of work items can always modify their own items regardless of role [S25]. Entities outside Projects (Programs, Users) use the account's Default Role.

16–17. REST API Model

The REST API is at https://<account>.tpondemand.com/api/v1/ [S8, S9]. Dev documentation redirects to IBM docs (403-blocked for anonymous callers). Public examples and GitHub gists confirm the following pattern:

Endpoint pattern

GET https://<account>.tpondemand.com/api/v1/<EntityType>
GET https://<account>.tpondemand.com/api/v1/<EntityType>/<id>
GET https://<account>.tpondemand.com/api/v1/Teams/<id>/TeamIterations

Known entity type resource names

UserStories, Features, Epics, PortfolioEpics, Bugs, Tasks, Requests, TestPlans, TestCases, Releases, Iterations, TeamIterations, Projects, Programs, Teams, Builds, Impediments, Milestones

Query parameters

ParameterPurposeExample
includeField projection + nested entity expansioninclude=[Name,Feature[Epic[Name]],Project]
whereOData-style filter (date, string, boolean)where=EndDate gt '2024-01-01'
appendComputed aggregate fieldsappend=[Tasks-Count,Bugs-Count]
orderbySortorderby=StartDate
takePagination limittake=50
skipPagination offsetskip=100
formatResponse formatformat=json

Hierarchy traversal via include

The include parameter supports multi-level nesting to traverse the hierarchy in a single request [S8, S9]:

GET /api/v1/UserStories?include=[Name,Effort,Feature[Name,Epic[Name,PortfolioEpic[Name]]]]

This returns each Story with its Feature, the Feature's Epic, and the Epic's Portfolio Epic in a single response. There is no dedicated "get full subtree from an Epic" endpoint; callers must start from the leaf (UserStories) and include upward, or start from the root and include downward collections (e.g. /api/v1/Epics?include=[Features[UserStories]]). Deep nesting of large trees will produce large response payloads.

Non-default fields

Fields not in the default response must be explicitly requested via include; omitting them returns a partial object silently with no error [S8].

18. Webhooks / Automation Rules

The original Webhooks plugin is being retired in favour of Automation Rules [S26, S27]. Automation Rules support:

  • Modified Entity source: triggers when a specific entity type is modified; configurable to fire on specific field changes (including parent FK changes, enabling hierarchy-change detection).
  • Incoming Webhook source: a unique HTTP endpoint that Targetprocess exposes, allowing external systems to trigger rules by POSTing JSON.
  • Time-based rules: cron-style scheduled triggers.
  • JavaScript: complex rules can use JavaScript for custom logic.

Outgoing payloads are sent to a configured URL. The detailed JSON payload schema is only documented in the IBM-hosted developer hub (403-blocked at crawl time); confirmed to include the modified entity object and changed field values based on Zapier integration metadata [S26, S27]. Automatic notifications also propagate to followers of parent entities — following a Feature auto-subscribes you to all child Story/Bug changes [S28].

Comparison: Targetprocess vs. VersionOne (Digital.ai Agility)

DimensionTargetprocess (IBM)VersionOne / Digital.ai Agility
Default work hierarchyPortfolio Epic → Epic → Feature → User Story → Task / BugPortfolio Item (Epic / Capability / Feature levels, configurable depth) → Story (Backlog Item) → Task / Test; Defect is a peer of Story
Portfolio items3 fixed levels: Portfolio Epic, Epic, FeatureFlexible: admins define the portfolio hierarchy levels; common default is Epic → Capability → Feature above Stories [S29, S30]
Project concept"Project" (sometimes labelled "Portfolio"); required container for all work; one Program per Project"Scope" (internal name); displayed as "Project" in UI; can be nested in a tree (child Scopes); primary work container [S31, S32]
Program conceptLightweight tag grouping multiple Projects; no nested projects"Program" is a cross-hierarchical collection of Projects for filtering/reporting; does not grant extra access; Programs are visible to all users but respect existing project permissions [S33]
Multi-projectSupported; entities can cross project boundaries; Programs span projectsSupported; Scope tree nesting allows parent/child project hierarchy; Programs are cross-cutting
API modelREST v1; per-type resource URLs (/api/v1/Epics); OData-style where/include/append; no generic entity endpointREST (/rest-1.v1/Data/<AssetType>); Meta API (/meta.v1); Bulk API; OID token asset IDs (Type:Integer); strong typing with abstract/concrete asset type hierarchy [S31, S34]
Custom entity typesNo new types; rename existing only; Custom Fields extend dataPortfolio Item levels can be configured (add/rename levels); standard types (Story, Defect, Task, Test) are fixed; Custom Fields supported [S29, S30]
Permissioning4 account types; project + team dual membership; role per membership; creator override; no row-level security beyond membershipRole-based; Members assigned to Scopes with a Role; Member roles can be per-scope; program visibility does not grant access; admin bulk-assign API available [S33, S35]
Webhooks / eventsAutomation Rules replacing Webhooks; entity field-change events; incoming + outgoing webhooksNative webhook API (/api/webhook); subscribes to AssetCreated and AssetChanged events; selective filtering by asset type [S34]
RollupsComputed on read; Progress = SUM(EffortCompleted)/SUM(Effort); Bug effort excluded from Story/Feature chainsEstimate/Actual aggregation via API query aggregation operators (@Sum, @Count); not explicitly documented as stored vs. computed

Gaps and Limitations

  • IBM documentation (403): All pages at ibm.com/docs/en/targetprocess returned HTTP 403 to unauthenticated crawlers. The authoritative IBM-era documentation is inaccessible without an IBM ID. Content was sourced from the public targetprocess.com/guide which appears to mirror or pre-date the IBM rehosting.
  • Developer hub (redirect): dev.targetprocess.com redirects to IBM docs (403). API details were obtained from GitHub gists, Stitchflow user management docs, and PyPI package docs — all secondary sources.
  • Storage model: The internal database schema is not published. Separate-table vs. single-table-polymorphic inference is based on API resource naming convention only.
  • Rollup: stored vs. computed: Documentation says aggregation is "automatic" but does not specify whether values are materialised in the database or computed on every read. The phrasing strongly implies on-read computation.
  • Automation Rules payload schema: Full JSON payload schema for outgoing webhook events is in the IBM-gated docs; not obtained.
  • VersionOne depth: The secondary pass is lighter; VersionOne's configurable portfolio hierarchy depth and exact permission matrix were not fully enumerable from available public sources.
  • IBM rebranding changes: It is unclear whether IBM has materially changed the Targetprocess data model post-acquisition (2023–2026) vs. the Apptio-era model documented at targetprocess.com/guide.
